title: Localhost ACL Repair And Successful Lifecycle Follow-up Run

sourceTasklist.goal: Run one reproducible Linux lifecycle test that covers installer or setup execution, localhost scaffold access, safe shutdown, uninstall, explicit data cleanup, and a performance report for each major step in the sequence.

executionShape.installerMode: local-checkout reuse with setup-time move into /opt/ehecatl

comparisonBaseline.repairedIssue: tenant ACL traversal for manager, engine, and tenant runtime users

summary.highLevelVerdict: The localhost scaffold path now works after the ACL repair. The repaired run served the documented localhost smoke routes successfully, including redirect, static asset, cached asset, plain controller, and session-capable controller responses. A second freshly created tenant host (`autofix.local`) also answered successfully, which shows the fix is not limited to one repaired localhost directory. Cleanup still benefits from an explicit PM2 registry delete in addition to uninstall and purge.

exact: Patched install log birth and modify timestamps from `/tmp/ehecatl-smoke-install-fixed.log`.
HTTP status codes and Date headers observed during successful localhost probes.
Final filesystem and PM2 end-state checks.
inferred: Boot-to-first-successful-response elapsed time is inferred from PM2 startup timestamps and second-granularity HTTP Date headers.
Per-route wall-clock latency totals were not persisted beyond curl success output, so no mean or p95 values are claimed here.

notes: This timing comes from the install log file birth and modify timestamps.
The machine already had Node.js, Redis, PM2, and warmed npm state from earlier validation, so this is a fast follow-up setup timing rather than a first-machine package-download timing.
Start time is the first PM2-backed main bootstrap timestamp after the repaired restart.
End time is the HTTP Date header on the first successful localhost `/hello` response after the repaired localhost scaffold and runtime restart.

firstSuccessfulResponse.note: The first confirmed successful localhost response arrived after the repaired runtime restart.

responseHighlights: Body contained the hello-world HTML page.
Body contained the cached example HTML page.

generalizationProbe.note: A newly created tenant host using the repaired `tenant_create` flow also answered successfully, which shows the ACL repair generalized beyond the manually rebuilt localhost host.

aclRepairEffectiveness.question: Did the ACL repair address the earlier localhost blocker in a durable way?
aclRepairEffectiveness.answer: Yes. The earlier failed lifecycle reported that the manager could not open `/var/opt/ehecatl/tenants/localhost/www/config.json` because the scaffolded host lacked usable traversal ACLs. The repaired `tenant_create` flow now grants directory search permission for manager and engine on the tenant host path and also grants the tenant process user traversal through the shared `/var/opt/ehecatl/.../<domain>` path. The same traversal repair was added to `proxy_setup` so later maintenance does not regress the filesystem state.

evidence.note: The previous run summary recorded the `EACCES` localhost failure.
The previous run captured the missing ACL entries on the scaffolded tenant host.
The scaffold command now resolves both the tenants base and the var base path from runtime policy.
The repair adds directory-search and path-traversal ACL helpers for role users.
The scaffold flow now grants tenant runtime traversal before applying manager and engine ACLs.
Proxy maintenance now re-applies the same traversal model for tenant users.
Proxy maintenance carries the same directory-search and role-ACL helpers as tenant scaffolding.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The manager can traverse and read the scaffold tenant host config path
The engine can traverse the scaffold tenant host asset path
The tenant process user can traverse the shared runtime path to its own host directory
The repair is reflected in both scaffolding and later proxy-maintenance code paths

localhostSmokeCriteria.question: Did the repaired runtime satisfy the documented localhost smoke path?
localhostSmokeCriteria.answer: Yes. The repaired follow-up run satisfied the route set described by the bundled template and the smoke criteria: `/` redirected to `/htm/index.htm`, `/htm/index.htm` and `/htm/cached.htm` returned the expected HTML pages, `/hello` returned JSON with `message` and `timestampUtc`, and `/session` returned JSON with `message`, `sessionId`, `sessionData`, and `timestampUtc`.

evidence.note: The smoke criteria define the required boot, redirect, asset, controller, session, and clean-stop behavior.
The bundled tenant template defines the localhost smoke routes and expected response shapes.
The scaffolded host config maps `/`, `/htm/*.htm`, `/hello`, and `/session` exactly as validated in the follow-up run.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The static asset route `/htm/index.htm` answered successfully
The cached static asset route `/htm/cached.htm` answered successfully
The plain controller route `/hello` answered successfully
The session-capable controller route `/session` answered successfully

capturedPerformanceData.question: What useful performance information was captured in the repaired follow-up run?
capturedPerformanceData.answer: The follow-up run captured one exact fast-path install timing and one approximate boot-to-first-successful-response timing. The patched follow-up install and scaffold completed in 2.780 seconds on an already-prepared machine. After the repaired restart, the first confirmed successful localhost response arrived roughly 11.5 seconds after main bootstrap began. What this report still does not provide is a full request-latency baseline with repeated samples, mean, or p95 for `/htm/index.htm`, `/hello`, and `/session`.

evidence.note: The baseline plan already calls for cold-start timing plus repeated latency measurements for the scaffold routes.
The packaged operational surface uses PM2 start, restart, stop, and the built-in test command used during validation.
The earlier failed lifecycle run captured exact phase timings but could not capture any successful localhost-response timing at all.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: An exact patched-install timing was captured
A first-successful-response timing was captured for localhost
Repeated request-latency samples with mean and p95 were captured
The repaired run is sufficient to prove the localhost path is no longer blocked

cleanupEndState.question: Did the follow-up run end in a clean operational state?
cleanupEndState.answer: Yes, but not through uninstall and purge alone. The final state was clean: `/opt/ehecatl`, `/etc/opt/ehecatl`, `/var/opt/ehecatl`, and `/srv/opt/ehecatl` were absent, and the PM2 registry was empty. As in the earlier failed lifecycle report, PM2 still benefited from an explicit `pm2 delete all` step beyond the packaged uninstall and purge scripts, because uninstall intentionally removes project files and metadata while purge intentionally removes only filesystem data.

evidence.note: Uninstall removes the CLI and project tree while intentionally preserving runtime data.
Purge is the separate explicit destructive step for `/etc`, `/var`, and `/srv` runtime data.
The packaged lifecycle exposes `start`, `restart`, `stop`, and `log`, but not a PM2 registry delete command.
The earlier run already observed that explicit PM2 deletion was needed for a blank registry.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The uninstall path removed the installed project tree
The purge path removed the preserved runtime data trees
The final filesystem state was clean
Uninstall and purge alone guarantee an empty PM2 registry

mustFix: Add a repeatable benchmark harness now that localhost smoke is working so future reports can capture repeated latency samples, mean, and p95 for `/htm/index.htm`, `/hello`, and `/session`.
shouldFix: Decide whether a packaged maintenance command should clear stale PM2 app entries so teardown leaves a blank PM2 registry as well as a blank filesystem.
Record first-successful-response timing and route-level wall-clock timings directly in future lifecycle logs instead of inferring them from PM2 timestamps and HTTP Date headers.
niceToHave: Capture the repaired lifecycle again on a fully fresh machine where package-install time is part of the measurement, not only the warmed follow-up setup path.
Compare cold-spawn versus warm-route timings for both localhost and a second newly created tenant host.
